senses.g: faultiness, unsoundness, imperfection, something amiss, fault, defect, blemish, infirmity, vice, crime, sin; disgrace, infamy

عید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِید ('īd), from Arabic عِيد (ʕīd).

عید الفطر.e: Borrowed from Arabic عِيد الْفِطْر (ʕīd al-fiṭr).

عیسوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِیسَوِی (īsawī), from Arabic عِيسَوِيّ (ʕīsawiyy, “of or relating to ʿĪsá, i.e. Jesus”).

ex.u: Coordinate term: قَبْلِ مَسِیح (qabl-i masīh)

عیسوی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِیسَوِی (īsawī), from Arabic عِيسَوِيّ (ʕīsawiyy, “of or relating to ʿĪsá, i.e. Jesus”).

senses.g: a male given name, Isa, from Arabic, of Muslim usage

عیسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عِیسَیٰ (īsā), borrowed from Arabic عِيسَى (ʕīsā).

عیش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَیْش (ayš), borrowed from Arabic عَيْش (ʕayš), from عَاشَ (ʕāša).

senses.g: to live a life of pleasure

عین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian عَیْن (ayn), from Arabic عَيْن (ʕayn).

senses.g: The twenty-fifth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by ع and followed by ف.
a type of tree in jungles and mountains.

غار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَار (ġār), borrowed from Arabic غَار (ḡār). First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi غار (ġar /⁠ġār⁠/).

غالب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَالِب (ġālib), from Arabic غَالِب (ḡālib).

senses.g: probably, apparently, for the most part, mostly, chiefly

غالبا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غالباً (ġâliban), from Arabic غَالِبًا (ḡāliban).

غالی.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

senses.g: gulp (the sound of swallowing a liquid substance)

غٹاغٹ.e: Possibly related to غٹ غٹ (ġaṭ ġaṭ, “gulp”), and influenced by فٹافٹ (faṭāfaṭ, “immediately”)

senses.g: the act of drinking without taking breaths

غٹاغٹ.e: Possibly related to غٹ غٹ (ġaṭ ġaṭ, “gulp”), and influenced by فٹافٹ (faṭāfaṭ, “immediately”)

غدار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَدّار, from Arabic غَدَّار (ḡaddār, “traitor, swindler”). First attested in c. 1665 as Middle Hindi غدار (ġdar).
Borrowed from Classical Persian غَدّار, from Arabic غَدَّار (ḡaddār, “traitor, swindler”). First attested in c. 1665 as Middle Hindi غدار (ġdar).

غذا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غذا (ġizā), from Arabic غِذَاء (ḡiḏāʔ).

غرض.e: First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi غَرَض (ġrẓ), borrowed from Classical Persian غَرَض (ġaraz), from Arabic غَرَض (ḡaraḍ).
First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi غَرَض (ġrẓ), borrowed from Classical Persian غَرَض (ġaraz), from Arabic غَرَض (ḡaraḍ).

غرق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَرْق (ġarq), from Arabic غَرْق (ḡarq).

senses.g: Granada (a city and province of Andalusia, Spain)

غروب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غروب (ġurūb), from Arabic غُرُوب (ḡurūb).

غرور.e: Borrowed from Persian غرور, from Arabic غُرُور (ḡurūr).

ex.u: وہ ایک غَرِیب لَکَڑْہارے سے لَکْڑی کَٹْوانے گَیا ہَے۔
ex.r: vo ek ġarīb lakaṛhāre se lakṛī kaṭvāne gayā hai.
ex.e: He went to get the wood cut by a poor carpenter.

غریب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَرِیب (ġarīb), borrowed from Arabic غَرِيب (ḡarīb), from غَرُبَ (ḡaruba).

senses.g: ghazal (a poetic form mostly used for love poetry in Middle Eastern, South, and Central Asian poetry)

غزل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَزَل (ġazal), from Arabic غَزَل (ḡazal).

senses.g: the battles in which Prophet Muhammad (SWT) was involved in

غزوہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غزوه (ġazwa), from Arabic غَزْوَة (ḡazwa).

senses.g: Gaza (a city in the Gaza Strip, Palestine in the Levant; in full, Gaza City)

غزہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غزه (ġazza), from Arabic غَزَّة (ḡazza).

senses.g: Gaza Strip (a region in Palestine between Egypt and Israel)

غژماں.e: Derived from Classical Persian غژم (ġužm, “anger, rage”).

غسل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غسل (ġusl), from Arabic غُسْل (ḡusl).

غسل خانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غسل خانه (ġusl-xāna), the first element from Arabic غُسْل (ḡusl). Compare Ottoman Turkish غسلخانه (gusülhane, “bathroom”).

senses.g: alternative form of غُسْل خانَہ (ġusl xāna)

غش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غش (ġaš), from Arabic غَشْي (ḡašy).

غصہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غصه (ġussa, “grief, sorrow”), from Arabic غُصّة (ḡuṣṣa, “distress, pang”). Compare Gujarati ગુસ્સો (gusso).

غضب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غضب (ġazab), from Arabic غَضَب (ḡaḍab).
Borrowed from Classical Persian غضب (ġazab), from Arabic غَضَب (ḡaḍab).

غفلت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَفْلَت (ġaflat), from Arabic غَفْلَة (ḡafla).

غلام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غُلَام (ġulām), from Arabic غُلَام (ḡulām). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi غلام (ġlam /⁠ġulām⁠/).

غلط.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَلَط (ġalat), borrowed from Arabic غَلَط (ḡalaṭ).

ex.u: شایَد آپ کو غَلَط فَہْمی ہُئی ہَے۔
ex.r: śāyad āp ko ġalat fahmī huī hai.
ex.e: Perhaps you are mistaken.

غلط فہمی.e: Compound of غَلَط (ġalat, “wrong, incorrect”) + فَہْم (fahm, “understanding”) + -ی (-ī, noun suffix), from Classical Persian غَلَط فَهْمِی (ġalat fahmī).

غلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غله (ġalla), from Arabic غَلَّة (ḡalla).

غلیظ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَلِیظ (ġalīz), from Arabic غَلِيظ (ḡalīẓ).

ex.u: اُس کی موت پر گہرا غم منایا گیا۔
ex.r: uskī maut par gahrā ġam manāyā gayā.
ex.e: His death was deeply mourned.

غم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَم (ġam), borrowed from Arabic غَمّ (ḡamm). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi غم (ġm /⁠ġam⁠/).

غمگین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَمگِین. By surface analysis, غم + ـگین (-gen).

ex.u:
غنچے نے تاج گل نے کیا پیرہن درست
شادی بہار کی ہے ہوا ہے چمن درست
ġuñce ne tāj gul ne kiyā pairhan durust
ex.r: śādī bahār kī hai huā hai caman durust
ex.e: (please add an English translation of this quotation)

غنڈہ.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀕𑁄𑀁𑀟 (goṃḍa) / गोंड (goṃḍa) / ಗೋಂಡ (goṃḍa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit गोण्ड (goṇḍa, “Gond”).

ex.u: اُس پر غنودگی طاری رہتی ہے، اور جب وہ سو جاتا ہے، تو وہ نیند میں بَرّانے لگتا ہے۔
ex.r: buxārõ kā usūl-i 'ilāj
ex.e: Drowsiness overcomes him, and when he falls asleep, he begins to mutter in his slumber.

غنودہ.e: From Classical Persian غُنُودَه (ġunūda), past participle of غنودن (ġonudan, “to nap; slumber; sleep”).

غنہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غنه (ġunna), from Arabic غُنَّة (ḡunna).

senses.g: a vowel sign to mark nasalisation

غنہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غنه (ġunna), from Arabic غُنَّة (ḡunna).

غنیمت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غَنِیمَت (ġanīmat), from Arabic غَنِيمَة (ḡanīma).

ex.u: اس فیصلے پر ہم نے کافی غورکیا۔
ex.r: is faisle par hamne kāfī ġaur kiyā.
ex.e: We put enough thought into this decision.

ex.u: غور سے سنو
ex.r: gaur se sunō
ex.e: Listen carefully

غور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غور (ġawr), from Arabic غَوْر (ḡawr, “bottom, depth, (fig.) deepness in thought”).

غوطہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غوطه, from Arabic غَوْطَة (ḡawṭa).

غیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غیر (ġayr), from Arabic غَيْر (ḡayr).

ex.u: غَیر آدمی
ex.r: ġair ādmī
ex.e: unrelated man

غیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غیر (ġayr), from Arabic غَيْر (ḡayr).

غیر ملکی.e: From غَیرـ (ġair-, “non-”) + مُلْکی (mulkī, “belonging to the country; indigenous, local”).

غیرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غیرت (ġayrat), from Arabic غَيْرَة (ḡayra).

senses.g: self-respecting, one with sense of honour

غیرت مند.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian غیرَت مَند.

senses.g: The twenty-sixth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by غ and followed by ق.

ex.u: شَہَنْشاہِ فارِس
ex.r: śahanśāh-i fāris
ex.e: Emperor of Persia

فارس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَارِس (fāris), from Arabic فَارِس (fāris), from Early New Persian پَارْس (pārs, “Persia, Persis”).

فارسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَارْسِی (fārsī), from Early New Persian پَارْسِی (pārsī), influenced by Arabic فَارِسِيّ (fārisiyy). Doublet of پارْسی (pārsī).

senses.g: a person of Persian ethnicity or heritage; a person from Persia

فارسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَارْسِی (fārsī), from Early New Persian پَارْسِی (pārsī), influenced by Arabic فَارِسِيّ (fārisiyy). Doublet of پارْسی (pārsī).

فارغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فارغ (fāriġ), from Arabic فَارِغ (fāriḡ). Compare Punjabi فارَغ (fāraġ) / ਫ਼ਾਰਗ਼ (fāraġ), Gujarati ફારગ (phārag).

senses.g: graduate (one who has completed a course of study)
free time, unoccupied time, break time
Fez (the capital city of the region of Fez-Meknes, Morocco)

فاصلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فاصله (fāsila), from Arabic فَاصِلَة (fāṣila).

senses.g: exceeding, being over and above, abundant, in excess, redundant, superfluous, supernumerary
excellent; virtuous; excellent in learning, learned, erudite; perfect, accomplished
excess, redundancy, superfluity; residue, remainder, surplus, balance; receipt in excess (of revenue, etc.); waste
a learned man, an erudite person, a perfect or accomplished scholar

فاقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فاقه (fāqa), from Arabic فَاقَة (fāqa).

فالج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَالَج (fālaj), from Arabic فَالَج (fālaj). First attested in c. 1665 as Middle Hindi فالج (falj).

فالحال.e: Univerbation of Arabic فِي ٱلْحَال (fī l-ḥāl, “immediately”, literally “in the state”).

فالودہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فالوده (fālūda), reborrowed from Arabic فَالُودَة (fālūda), from Middle Persian [script needed] (pʾlwtk' /⁠pālūdag⁠/, “starch jelly, flummery”, past participle), from Middle Persian [script needed] (pʾlwtn' /⁠pālūdan⁠/, “to strain, filter, purify”). Cognate with Bengali ফালুদা (phaluda), Gujarati ફાલુદા (phāludā) and Punjabi ਫ਼ਲੂਦਾ (falūdā).

فانوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَانُوس (fānūs, “lantern”), itself from Arabic فَانُوس (fānūs, “lantern”).

فانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَانِی (fānī), borrowed from Arabic فَانٍ (fānin), from فَنِيَ (faniya), from Proto-Semitic *pan-. First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi فانی (fany /⁠fānī⁠/).

فائدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَایِدَه (fāyida), borrowed from Arabic فَائِدَة (fāʔida), from فَادَ (fāda).

فتح.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَتْح (fath), from Arabic فَتْح (fatḥ).

فتحپور.e: From فَتْح (fath, “victory”) + ـپُور (-pūr, “city or town”), literally “city or town of victory”.

فتنہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِتْنَه (fitna), from Arabic فِتْنَة (fitna).

فتور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فتور (futūr), from Arabic فُتُور (futūr). Cognate with Bengali ফিতুর (phitur).

فتیلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فتیله, from Arabic فَتِيلَة (fatīla).

senses.g: immediately, quickly, swiftly, speedily, in no time

فٹافٹ.e: Borrowed from Punjabi فَٹافَٹ (faṭāfaṭ) / ਫਟਾਫਟ (phaṭāphaṭ). Phonetic changes of /pʰ/ to /f/ usually do not occur in Urdu (unlike Hindi and Punjabi) onomatopoeic. Related to پھٹ (phaṭ, “bang”).

senses.g: Fiji (a country and archipelago of over 300 islands in Melanesia in Oceania)

فدیہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فدیه (fidya), from Arabic فِدْيَة (fidya).

senses.g: Euphrates (a river in West Asia, 2780 kilometers in length, flowing southwest from Turkey, then southeast, and uniting with the Tigris before entering the Persian Gulf)

فرات.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فرات (furāt), from Arabic الْفُرَات (al-furāt), ultimately from Akkadian 𒀀𒇉𒌓𒄒𒉣 (^(ÍD)Purattu).

فراز.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فراز (farāz, “height; tall”), from Middle Persian.
Borrowed from Classical Persian فراز (farāz, “height; tall”), from Middle Persian.
Borrowed from Classical Persian فراز (farāz, “height; tall”), from Middle Persian.
Borrowed from Classical Persian فراز (farāz, “height; tall”), from Middle Persian.

senses.g: anxiety, grief, regret (on account of absence)

فراق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِرَاق (firāq), borrowed from Arabic فِرَاق (firāq), from فَارَقَ (fāraqa). First attested in c. 1421 as Middle Hindi فراق (fraq /⁠firāq⁠/).

senses.g: France (a country located primarily in Western Europe)

فرانسیسی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فراسیسی (farāsīsī). Compare Gujarati ફરાસીસી (pharāsīsī), Bengali ফরাসি (phoraśi).
Borrowed from Classical Persian فراسیسی (farāsīsī). Compare Gujarati ફરાસીસી (pharāsīsī), Bengali ফরাসি (phoraśi).
Borrowed from Classical Persian فراسیسی (farāsīsī). Compare Gujarati ફરાસીસી (pharāsīsī), Bengali ফরাসি (phoraśi).

فربہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَرْبِهْ (farbih), from Middle Persian [script needed] (plpyh /⁠frabīh⁠/), from Old Persian *frapīδwa-, from Proto-Iranian *fra- + *piHtú (“fat, tallow”).

فردوس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فردوس (firdaws), from Arabic فِرْدَوْس (firdaws), ultimately from Proto-Iranian *paridayjah.

فرزند.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فرزند (farzand), ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *ǵenh₁-.

فرش.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فرش (farš), from Arabic فَرْش (farš, “carpet, rug”).

senses.g: a saviour from God in the form of an angel

فرشتہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِرِشْتَه (firišta). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi فرشتہ (frśth /⁠firiśta⁠/).

فرصت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فُرْصَت (fursat), borrowed from Arabic فُرْصَة (furṣa). First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi فرصت (frṣt).

فرض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَرْض (farz), from Arabic فَرْض (farḍ). First attested in c. 1534 as Middle Hindi فرض (farz).

senses.g: fard, a religious duty or a divine command
right (often moral but not limited to, also legal)
rightful inheritance (ie. the right of inheritors to inherit the deceased's wealth)

فرض.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَرْض (farz), from Arabic فَرْض (farḍ). First attested in c. 1534 as Middle Hindi فرض (farz).

فرضی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فرضی (farzī), from Arabic فَرَضِيّ (faraḍiyy).

فرعون.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِرْعَوْن (fir'awn), borrowed from Arabic فِرْعَوْن (firʕawn), possibly borrowed from Classical Syriac ܦܹܪܥܘܼܢ, borrowed from Koine Greek Φαραών (Pharaṓn), borrowed from Biblical Hebrew פַּרְעֹה, borrowed from Egyptian pr-ꜥꜣ, from pr + ꜥꜣ.

فرقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِرْقَه (firqa), from Arabic فِرْقَة (firqa).

senses.g: a firman, decree; mandate (especially a royal or an official one)

فرمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَرْمَان (farmān). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi فَرْمَان (frman). Doublet of پْرَمَان (pramān).

ex.u: مَیں قائِد کا فَرْمَان۔ میرے سِینے میں قُرْآن
ex.r: ma͠i qāid kā farmān. mere sīne mẽ qur-ān
ex.e: I am servant of my leader, with the Word of God lying against my heart; chest.

فرمان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَرْمَان (farmān). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi فَرْمَان (frman). Doublet of پْرَمَان (pramān).

senses.g: to bequest, request; to utter, say graciously

ex.u: درست فرمایا
ex.r: durust farmāyā
ex.e: rightly said

senses.g: to say, go ahead; used to show respect

ex.u: فرمائیے
ex.r: farmāiye
ex.e: go ahead (frequently used on the telephone)

senses.g: In compound verbs, used as a substitute for کرنا (karnā, “to do”), especially in the imperative, to show respect

فرمانا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فرما (farmā) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

فرنگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فرنگی (farangī), from Old French franc.
Borrowed from Classical Persian فرنگی (farangī), from Old French franc.

فروری.e: Borrowed from English February, possibly influenced by Portuguese fevereiro. Compare Classical Persian فَبْرَوِی (fabrawī), Konkani फेवरेर (phevrer).

senses.g: a male given name, Farhad, from Persian
a male given name, Farhad, from Persian — Farhad (the unrequited lover of Queen Shirin in the romance Khosrow and Shirin.)

فرہنگ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَرْهَنْگ (farhang, “dictionary; knowledge”).

senses.g: cry; shout, crying out for help or succour
a female given name, Faryal or Farial, from Persian, meaning “the one with a beautiful neck, good neck”
used a suffix to indicate temptation

فریب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فریب (fareb, firēb). First attested in c. 1692 as Middle Hindi فریب (fryb).

senses.g: Frankfurt, Frankfurt-am-Main (the largest city in Hesse, in central Germany)
Frankfurt, Frankfurt-an-der-Oder (a sizable town in Brandenburg, in eastern Germany)

فزان.e: Borrowed from Arabic فَزَّان (fazzān), from Latin Phazania.

فسانہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فسانه (fasāna). Doublet of افسانہ (afsāna).

ex.u: فَصْل کاٹْنا
ex.r: fasl kāṭnā
ex.e: to harvest the crops

فصل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَصْل (fasl), borrowed from Arabic فَصْل (faṣl). Cognate with Punjabi ਫ਼ਸਲ (fasal), Bengali ফসল (phośol), Assamese ফচল (phosol).

ex.u: کُھلی فَضا میں چَہَل قَدْمی کَریں۔
ex.r: khulī fazā mẽ cahal qadmī karẽ.
ex.e: Stroll in the open air.

فضا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَضَا (fazā), from Arabic فَضَاء (faḍāʔ)

فضائیہ.e: Borrowed from Persian فضایی (fazâyi), from Arabic فَضَائِيّ (faḍāʔiyy).

فضل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَضْل (fazl), borrowed from Arabic فَضْل (faḍl). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi فضل (fẓl /⁠fazl⁠/).

فضول.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فُضُول (fuzūl), borrowed from Arabic فُضُول (fuḍūl), from فَضَلَ (faḍala). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi فضول (fẓvl /⁠fuzūl⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian فُضُول (fuzūl), borrowed from Arabic فُضُول (fuḍūl), from فَضَلَ (faḍala). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi فضول (fẓvl /⁠fuzūl⁠/).

فطرت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فطرت (fitrat), from Arabic فِطْرَة (fiṭra, “human nature”).

فعل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِعْل (fi'l), from Arabic فِعْل (fiʕl).

فعل متعدی.e: Borrowed from Arabic فِعْل مُتَعَدٍّ (fiʕl mutaʕaddin).

senses.g: indeed, in fact, actually, really, practically

فغاں.e: Variant of اَفْغَان (afġān), from Classical Persian اَفْغَان (afġān).

فقرہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِقْرَه (fiqra), from Arabic فِقْرَة (fiqra).

senses.g: inflection of فِقْرَہ (fiqra, “line; phrase”): — direct plural
inflection of فِقْرَہ (fiqra, “line; phrase”): — oblique/vocative singular

فقہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فقه (fiqh), from Arabic فِقْه (fiqh).

فقیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَقِیر (faqīr), from Arabic فَقِير (faqīr).

فکر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِکْر (fikr), from Arabic فِكْر (fikr). First attested as Old Hindi फिकरि (phikari).

فلاں.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فلان (fulān), from Arabic فُلَان (fulān).

senses.g: flight (a trip made by an aircraft)

ex.u: جزائر سماترا اور فلپائن اسپین سے متعلق ہیں۔
ex.r: Munshī Naval Kishor
ex.e: The Islands of Sumatra and the Philippines are subordinate to Spain.

senses.g: Palestine (the region in Western Asia in the Middle East between the Mediterranean Sea and the Jordan River)
Palestine (a country in Western Asia, in the Middle East; the State of Palestine; the homeland of the Palestinian people)

فلسطین.e: Borrowed from Arabic فِلَسْطِين (filasṭīn), likely influenced by Classical Persian فلسطین (falastīn). Compare Punjabi فَلَسْطِین (falast̤īn).

فلک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فلک (falak), from Arabic فَلَك (falak, “[celestial] orb, [celestial] sphere”).

فلکیات.e: From either Arabic فَلَك (falak, “[celestial] orbs, astronomy”) or simply فلک (falak, “heaven, sky”) + ـیات (-yāt).

فن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَن (fan), from Arabic فَنّ (fann), from earlier Persian پند (pand).

فناہ ہونا.e: From فناہ (fanā, “die”) + ہونا (honā, “to be”), literally “to die”.

senses.g: a horde (both in terms of items or people).

فوج.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَوْج (fawj), borrowed from Arabic فَوْج (fawj), from فَيْج (fayj), borrowed from Middle Persian pdk'. First attested in c. 1503 as Middle Hindi فوج (fvj /⁠fauj⁠/).

ex.u: فوجداری قانون
ex.r: fojdārī qānon
ex.e: criminal law

senses.g: immediately, instantly, urgently, promptly, rapidly, instantaneously

فورا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فورا (fawran), from Arabic فَوْرًا (fawran).

senses.g: immediate, urgent, prompt, rapid, instantaneous, instant

ex.u: میرے بھائی کا فُون آیا ہَے۔
ex.r: mere bhāī kā fūn āyā hai.
ex.e: I got a phone call from my brother.

فون.e: Borrowed from English phone, from telephone, borrowed from French téléphone, from télé- + -phone.

senses.g: to phone, to telephone, to call, to contact via telephone

فہرست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فهرست (fihrist). Compare Sindhi فهرست (fihrista).

فہم.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَهم, from Arabic فَهْم (fahm).

senses.g: Ferozewala (a city in Sheikhupura district, Punjab, Pakistan)

فیروزی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فیروزی (fērōzī, “turquoise-coloured”), from Arabic فَيْرُوز (fayrūz), from earlier Persian پیروزه (pērōza, “turquoise”).

ex.u: صد فیصد
ex.r: sad fīsad
ex.e: hundred percent

فیصد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِیصَد (fīsad, “per hundred”), from صَد (sad, “hundred”).

senses.g: a male given name, Faisal, from Arabic
Faisalabad (a city in Faisalabad district, Punjab, Pakistan). It is the third largest city in Pakistan.

فیصلہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَیْصَلَه (faysala), from Arabic فَيْصَلَة (fayṣala). Compare Bengali ফয়সালা (foẏsala).

فیضان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَیضَان (fayzān), from Arabic فَيْضَان (fayḍān). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi فیضان (fyẓan).

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

فیضان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فَیضَان (fayzān), from Arabic فَيْضَان (fayḍān). First attested in c. 1635 as Middle Hindi فیضان (fyẓan).

ex.u: اِس تَرازُو میں بَرابَر ہَیں وہ نیتا ہُوں کِہ فِیل۔ کھاتے ہَیں جَنتا کا حِصَّہ بھی یہ جَنتا کے وَکِیل۔
ex.r: is tarāzū mẽ barābar ha͠i vo netā hū̃ ki fīl. khāte ha͠i jantā kā hissa bhī ye jantā ke vakīl.
ex.e: The politician and the elephant are equal by this measure / This representative of the people devours even the public treasure

ex.u: سَجے ہُوئے ہَیں پیادَہ و اَسْپ و فِیل تَمام۔ بِچھی ہُوئی ہَے بِساط جَہاں کَہاں گَیا مَیں۔
ex.r: saje hūe ha͠i pyāda o asp o fīl tamām. bichī hūī hai bisāt jahā̃ kahā̃ gayā ma͠i.
ex.e: All the pawn and knight and bishop (or alfil) are set / Where the chess cloth is spread / Where have I gone?

فیل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فیل (fīl), from Arabic فِيل (fīl), from Middle Persian 𐭯𐭩𐭫 (pyl /⁠pīl⁠/). Doublet of پِیل (pīl).

فیل مرغ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فِیلمُرْغ (fīl-murġ). By surface analysis, فیل (fīl, “elephant”) + مرغ (murġ, “fowl”). Possibly a doublet of پِیرُو (pīrū).

فیلان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian فیلان (fīlān) from Arabic فِيلَان (fīlān), dual of فِيل (fīl) and used to refer to the two bishops in chess together.

فیلہ.e: Derived from Classical Persian فیلان (fīlān) from Arabic فِيلَان (fīlān), dual of فِيل (fīl) and used to refer to the two bishops in chess together. By surface analysis, فیل (fīl) + ـہ (-ah).
